header.headline: Diplomatic Legalization: Three Paths
header.description: The correct legalization path depends on the country receiving the document. Use this guide to identify which process applies to your situation.

intro: Document legalization is the process of having a document officially recognized for use in a foreign country. The process varies depending on whether the receiving country is a member of the Hague Convention.

paths.name: Consular Authentication
paths.subtitle: For documents going to Hungary
paths.usedFor: Hungarian authorities, property transactions, legal submissions
steps: Apostille from NH Secretary of State

paths.name: Apostille + Authentication
paths.subtitle: For Hague Convention countries
paths.usedFor: EU countries, most of Europe, 120+ countries
steps: Apostille from the Secretary of State of the issuing state
Consular authentication if required by destination country

paths.name: Full Chain Legalization
paths.subtitle: For non-Hague countries
paths.usedFor: Countries not party to the Hague Convention (certain Middle East, Asian, African nations)
steps: Consular authentication by destination country's embassy

items.question: Does Hungary require an apostille on American documents?
items.answer: Yes — Hungary is a party to the Hague Convention, so most American documents require an apostille before consular authentication. Get the apostille from the Secretary of State of the state where the document was issued.

items.question: Can the Honorary Consul do the apostille?
items.answer: No. Apostille is issued by the state Secretary of State (NH Secretary of State for New Hampshire documents). The Honorary Consul authenticates after the apostille is obtained.

items.question: Do Hungarian documents need legalization for use in the US?
items.answer: Hungarian documents used in the US may need consular authentication. Since Hungary is a Hague Convention member, Hungarian documents for the US typically require an apostille issued by Hungarian authorities.

items.question: How long does the full legalization process take?
items.answer: For Hungary: apostille takes 1–3 weeks from the Secretary of State, then the consular appointment is same-day. Plan for 2–4 weeks total. For non-Hague countries, allow 4–8 weeks or more.

cta.heading: Need Document Legalization?
cta.body: Book an appointment for consular authentication — the final step in the legalization chain for most Hungarian submissions.
